threats.title: Critical TVL Inflation via Aggregate Uniswap V3 Pool Position
threats.hypothesis_description: UNIv3Connector calculates TVL using the aggregate liquidity of the NonfungiblePositionManager for a tick range, rather than the specific NFT's liquidity, drastically inflating the vault's TVL.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `pool.positions(key)` call uses the `positionManager` address as the owner. This returns the sum of all liquidity provided through the manager at those ticks, not just the vault's share. This allows attackers to inflate the share price by adding liquidity to the same tick range.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.positions(key) queried using positionManager as owner instead of specific tokenId

preconditions: Vault holds a Uniswap V3 position
Other users provide liquidity through the same PositionManager at the same tick range

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Uses positionManager address instead of vault/tokenId for position lookup

analysis_questions: Does the key generation accurately isolate the vault's liquidity?
Can an attacker manipulate the aggregate liquidity to extract value?
validation_requirements: Add liquidity from a different address to the same tick range
Observe if the vault's TVL increases

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits into vault. Attacker then adds massive liquidity to the same Uniswap V3 pool and tick range via the PositionManager. Vault TVL artificially spikes. Attacker withdraws from vault, receiving more underlying assets than deposited.
prerequisites: Capital to provide Uniswap V3 liquidity

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Severe loss of funds due to share price manipulation

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for TVL calculation when multiple users provide liquidity to the same ticks

threats.title: Cross-Vault Privilege Escalation via Global Flash Loan Address
threats.hypothesis_description: The global flashLoan address in PositionRegistry grants manager access to all vaults. Combined with arbitrary external calls in BalancerFlashLoan, a keeper for one vault can drain funds from any other vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: NoyaGovernanceBase.onlyManager allows `msg.sender == registry.flashLoan()`. BalancerFlashLoan allows the caller (keeper) to execute arbitrary calls to any `destinationConnector` without validating the connector belongs to the keeper's vault. This completely breaks vault isolation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.sender == registry.flashLoan() grants access across all vaults

preconditions: Multiple vaults exist in the registry
Attacker controls the keeper multisig for at least one vault

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: destinationConnector[i].call executes without validating the target
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-vault isolation for flash loan execution

analysis_questions: Can a keeper for Vault A execute manager functions on Vault B's connectors?
Are destinationConnectors validated against the caller's vaultId?
validation_requirements: Setup two vaults with different keepers
Use Vault A's keeper to initiate a flash loan targeting Vault B's connector
Verify if Vault B's funds can be extracted

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Keeper of Vault A initiates a flash loan, passing Vault B's connector as the destination. The flash loan contract calls Vault B's connector. The connector sees the caller is the global flash loan address and grants manager access. Vault B's funds are drained.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total drain of all vaults in the protocol

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing cross-vault isolation tests for flash loan execution

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via unexpected connector calls

threats.title: Access Control Bypass in onlyVaultMaintainer Modifier
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyVaultMaintainer modifier uses logical OR (||) instead of AND (&&) in its revert condition, requiring the caller to possess BOTH the maintainer role and the EMERGENCY_ROLE to execute functions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The condition `if (msg.sender != maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false)` reverts if the sender is not the maintainer OR does not have the emergency role. This means only an address that is BOTH the maintainer AND has the emergency role can pass, bricking core configuration functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect boolean logic in access control modifier

preconditions: Maintainer attempts to configure the vault
Maintainer does not also hold the EMERGENCY_ROLE

analysis_questions: Can the maintainer call addConnector without the emergency role?
Is this logic consistent with other modifiers?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call addConnector as maintainer

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The protocol maintainer attempts to add a new connector or update trusted tokens but the transaction reverts. The vault cannot be configured or updated, leading to a denial of service for protocol administration.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inability to manage vaults, potentially locking funds if emergency updates are needed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or granting emergency role to maintainer

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for maintainer access control without emergency role

threats.title: State Corruption in Holding Position Swap-and-Pop
threats.hypothesis_description: When removing a holding position, the swap-and-pop logic updates the isPositionUsed mapping using calculatorConnector instead of the original ownerConnector, corrupting the mapping for token positions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Positions are added using `msg.sender` in the key. During removal, the swapped element's key is recalculated using `calculatorConnector`. For token positions, these are different (connector vs accountingManager), causing the mapping to store the wrong key and breaking future position lookups.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Key recalculation uses different fields than creation

preconditions: A holding position is removed
The removed position is not the last element in the array
The swapped element is a token position (type 0)

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that the recalculated key matches the original

analysis_questions: Does the key change during swap-and-pop for token positions?
Can the swapped position be accessed or removed afterward?
validation_requirements: Attempt to access or remove the second one
Verify it fails or creates duplicates

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Normal protocol operations cause a position removal. The last element (a token position) is swapped. Its index mapping is corrupted. The connector can no longer update or remove this token position, eventually exhausting maxNumHoldingPositions and freezing the vault.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Vault operations freeze due to max positions limit

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for position removal when multiple different position types exist

threats.title: Systemic DoS via TVL Calculation Underflow in Lending Connectors
threats.hypothesis_description: Multiple lending connectors calculate TVL by subtracting debt from collateral without checking for underflow. If a position becomes underwater, the subtraction reverts, breaking the entire vault's TVL calculation.
threats.investigation_rationale: In Solidity 0.8.x, `totalCollateral - totalDebt` reverts if debt > collateral. Since `TVLHelper.getTVL` iterates over all positions, a single underwater position will cause the global TVL calculation to revert, freezing all deposits, withdrawals, and fee calculations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unchecked subtraction of debt from collateral

preconditions: Vault has an active borrow position
Market conditions cause debt value to exceed collateral value

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No fallback to return 0 if debt > collateral

analysis_questions: Do lending connectors handle underwater positions gracefully?
Does a revert in one connector break the global TVL loop?
validation_requirements: Mock an oracle price drop causing debt > collateral

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During market volatility, a vault's position in Silo or Compound becomes undercollateralized. The TVL calculation reverts. Users panic and try to withdraw, but withdrawals revert because they depend on TVL. The vault is completely frozen.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires market recovery or contract upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for TVL calculation with underwater positions

threats.title: Disabled Routes Remain Executable Due to Logic Error
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyExistingRoute modifier uses AND (&&) logic, meaning it only reverts if a route is BOTH disabled AND has a zero address. Disabled routes with valid addresses can still be executed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The condition `if (routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled)` fails to block execution if `isEnabled` is false but `route` is not address(0). This renders the emergency disable mechanism completely ineffective.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect boolean logic in validation modifier

preconditions: A route is disabled by the maintainer
The route still has a non-zero address

analysis_questions: Can executeSwap be called with a disabled route?
Does the modifier correctly block disabled routes?
validation_requirements: Attempt to execute a swap using that route

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A swap route is found to be compromised. The maintainer disables it via setEnableRoute(false). An attacker or compromised keeper continues to route funds through the compromised route because the modifier fails to block it.
prerequisites: A disabled route with a non-zero address

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of funds through compromised or deprecated routes

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for executing swaps on disabled routes

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via usage of disabled routes

threats.title: Missing Withdrawal Validation in Watcher Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: The Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity function is completely empty, providing no validation when the accounting manager extracts tokens from connectors.
threats.investigation_rationale: BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress relies on the Watcher contract to verify that the `newAmount` requested by the accounting manager is safe and authorized. Because the function body is empty, any arbitrary amount passes validation, completely bypassing this security layer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Empty function body for critical security check

preconditions: Accounting manager requests token withdrawal from a connector

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No revert conditions exist in the verification function
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing logic to validate withdrawal amounts

analysis_questions: Does the Watcher contract provide any actual security?
Can the accounting manager extract arbitrary amounts?
validation_requirements: Request extraction of all connector funds
Verify the Watcher does not block it

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A bug or compromise in the accounting manager allows it to request more tokens than a user is entitled to. The connector calls the Watcher to verify the amount. The Watcher silently approves it. The connector sends the excessive amount, draining the vault.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Bypass of defense-in-depth, increasing impact of other bugs

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying that Watchers actually block invalid withdrawals

threats.title: Arbitrary External Calls via Flash Loan Receiver
threats.hypothesis_description: BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan executes arbitrary calls to destinationConnector addresses without validating that they are trusted protocol connectors.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function loops over `destinationConnector` array provided in `userData` and executes `.call{gas: gas[i]}(callingData[i])`. Only the `receiver` address is validated against the registry. A compromised keeper can use this to call any external contract with any calldata.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Arbitrary external call without target validation

preconditions: Keeper initiates a flash loan
Keeper provides malicious destinationConnector addresses in userData

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: destinationConnector[i] is not checked against registry.isAnActiveConnector
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing validation loop for destination connectors

analysis_questions: Can the keeper execute calls to non-connector contracts?
Can this be used to bypass protocol invariants?
validation_requirements: Pass an arbitrary contract (e.g., ERC20 token) as destinationConnector

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised keeper multisig initiates a flash loan. In the userData, it specifies a malicious contract or an ERC20 token as the destinationConnector. The flash loan contract executes the call, potentially approving tokens or interacting with unaudited protocols.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Expansion of keeper attack surface, potential loss of funds

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests validating flash loan execution targets

threats.title: Native ETH Permanently Stuck in SwapAndBridgeHandler
threats.hypothesis_description: The executeSwap and executeBridge functions are payable but fail to forward msg.value to the underlying implementation contracts, causing native ETH to be permanently stuck.
threats.investigation_rationale: When users or keepers send native ETH to execute a swap/bridge, the external call to `ISwapAndBridgeImplementation(swapImplInfo.route).performSwapAction(...)` does not include `{value: msg.value}`. The ETH remains in the handler contract, which has no withdrawal mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing {value: msg.value} in external call from payable function

preconditions: Caller sends native ETH to executeSwap or executeBridge

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: ETH is accepted but not passed to the router
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No rescue function in the handler contract

analysis_questions: Is msg.value forwarded anywhere in the execution path?
Can the stuck ETH be recovered?
validation_requirements: Check the ETH balance of the handler contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A keeper attempts to swap native ETH for a token. The transaction succeeds, but the ETH is not sent to the LiFi router. The swap fails at the router level, or the ETH is simply left in the handler contract. The funds are permanently lost.
prerequisites: Execution of swap/bridge using native ETH

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of native ETH used in swaps/bridges

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for native ETH swaps and bridges

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via increasing ETH balance in handler

threats.title: Borrow Positions Untracked Due to Incorrect Registry Update
threats.hypothesis_description: DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition calls registry.updateHoldingPosition with the removePosition flag set to true, failing to track newly opened borrow positions.
threats.investigation_rationale: When opening a position, the connector should add it to the registry. However, the last argument to `updateHoldingPosition` is `true` (removePosition). This means the position is never added to the tracking array, leading to inaccurate TVL and lost position data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect boolean flag passed to state update function

preconditions: Keeper opens a borrow position in Dolomite

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Passes true instead of false for removePosition

analysis_questions: Are Dolomite borrow positions tracked in the registry?
Does TVL calculation miss these positions?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Keeper opens a borrow position. The position is not tracked. The vault's TVL does not reflect the borrowed assets or debt. Share prices become inaccurate, allowing arbitrage by depositors/withdrawers.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inaccurate share pricing leading to value extraction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires manual intervention to fix registry state

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests verifying registry state after opening borrow positions

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via mismatched protocol vs registry state

threats.title: TVL Inflation via Double Counting of Synthetix Collateral
threats.hypothesis_description: SNXConnector calculates TVL by adding totalDeposited and totalAssigned. Since totalDeposited already includes totalAssigned, this double-counts delegated collateral, inflating the vault's TVL.
threats.investigation_rationale: In Synthetix V3, `totalDeposited` represents the total collateral in the account, while `totalAssigned` is the portion delegated to pools. Adding them together (`totalDeposited + totalAssigned`) counts the delegated portion twice.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect arithmetic formula for TVL calculation

preconditions: Vault has delegated collateral in Synthetix V3

analysis_questions: Does totalDeposited include totalAssigned in SNX V3?
How much is the TVL inflated by?
validation_requirements: Deposit and delegate collateral in SNX via connector
Check reported TVL vs actual collateral value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault delegates collateral to an SNX pool. The TVL immediately jumps because the delegated collateral is counted twice. Existing depositors withdraw at the inflated share price, stealing value from remaining depositors.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of funds due to inflated share price

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying TVL accuracy after SNX delegation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via sudden TVL spikes upon delegation

threats.title: Cross-Chain TVL Staleness Checks Defeated by Default Timestamps
threats.hypothesis_description: TVLHelper.getLatestUpdateTime returns type(uint256).max if positions are initialized with the default timestamp, defeating any staleness checks for cross-chain TVL data.
threats.investigation_rationale: New holding positions are initialized with `positionTimestamp = type(uint256).max`. `getLatestUpdateTime` finds the minimum timestamp across all positions. If all positions have the default value, it returns `max`, which will pass any `timestamp > block.timestamp - threshold` freshness checks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Minimum search over array containing type(uint256).max

preconditions: Vault relies on cross-chain TVL updates
Same-chain positions use default timestamps

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Returns max instead of block.timestamp when no valid timestamps exist
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing check to ignore type(uint256).max during minimum search

analysis_questions: Does the accounting manager rely on getLatestUpdateTime for staleness checks?
Can stale cross-chain TVL data be used to manipulate share prices?
validation_requirements: Add a position with default timestamp

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: LayerZero messaging fails, causing cross-chain TVL data to become stale. The accounting manager checks freshness using getLatestUpdateTime. Because same-chain positions have max timestamps, the check passes. Attackers exploit the stale TVL to arbitrage share prices.
prerequisites: Delayed or failed cross-chain TVL update

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Arbitrage opportunities leading to value extraction

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for TVL staleness checks with default timestamps
